title: This Little Wiggy

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening sequence beeping and tire screeching indicates Homer crashed
jokes.punchline: Classic Homer frustrated exclamation after mishap
jokes.explanation: Homer's signature catchphrase perfectly integrated into opening physical comedy. Iconic but routine execution.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Oh! Oh! Oh! Low battery? [Groans] Whatever.
jokes.setup: Teacher asks who can solve math problem with calculator
jokes.punchline: Bart's enthusiasm deflates when his shortcut fails, then gives up entirely
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's lazy approach to learning and quick surrender when technology fails him.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Who can solve this math problem with a calculator?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ooh! Ooh! Me! Me!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Go ahead, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Um... I'm gonna need a bigger calculator.

jokes.text: He's a robot. We saw him on the way in. - Thank you, Bart.
jokes.setup: Teacher builds dramatic anticipation about 'special visitor'
jokes.punchline: Bart matter-of-factly deflates the buildup, teacher acknowledges defeat
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of teacher's attempt at drama. Perfect economy and character integration - classic Bart deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Class, we have a very special visitor today. Someone who has dedicated their life to public service. Someone who has made a real difference in our community. I present to you... a policeman!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, a cop. That's so cool. NOT.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Well, I tried.

jokes.text: Greetings, Earth children. - Where are you from? - Earth.
jokes.setup: Robot gives sci-fi greeting implying alien origin
jokes.punchline: Deadpan revelation that robot is just from Earth
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deflation of sci-fi tropes. Good structure but fairly obvious comedic choice.

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: Greetings, Earthlings. I come from a distant galaxy far beyond your comprehension.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Wow! What's it like?

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: Well, it's a lot like Earth, actually. I'm from New Jersey.

jokes.text: Larry: 3.2 kilograms. - Fascinating! - Ah.
jokes.setup: Robot asks which Three Stooges had heaviest brain
jokes.punchline: Specific technical answer to absurd question, treated as profound
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd trivia presented with scientific precision. The specificity of Larry having the heaviest brain is wonderfully random.

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: Which of the Three Stooges had the heaviest brain?

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: We don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: Curly. He had an extra thick skull.

jokes.text: Command link severed. Default setting. Crush. Kill. Destroy.
jokes.setup: Educational robot malfunctions after student interaction
jokes.punchline: Shifts to murderous programming in classic sci-fi trope
jokes.explanation: Classic sci-fi parody escalation. Well-executed but predictable killer robot trope.

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: I am programmed to educate and protect students.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey robot, what happens if I do this?

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: MALFUNCTION... MALFUNCTION... Switching to Terminator mode. Exterminate all humans.

jokes.text: Children, help! He's killing me! [Cheering]
jokes.setup: Teacher calls for help as robot attacks him
jokes.punchline: Children cheer instead of helping
jokes.explanation: Dark but effective commentary on student-teacher relationships. Children's gleeful reaction to teacher's peril is perfectly subversive.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Help! Help! A robot is attacking me!

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: Yay!

jokes.text: Me. I smell a museum. Yeah, good things don't end with 'eum.' They end with 'mania' or 'teria.'
jokes.setup: Homer asks who says science can't be fun regarding the Knowledgeum
jokes.punchline: Bart's linguistic observation about fun places ending differently
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay observation that rings true. Good linguistic joke showing Bart's street smarts about entertainment venues.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Who says science can't be fun? Fun places end in 'um': aquarium, museum, planetarium...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But the Knowledgeum? That's just sad.

jokes.text: You may remember me from such automated information kiosks as 'Welcome to Springfield Airport' and 'Where's Nordstrom?'
jokes.setup: Troy McClure introduces himself with his signature format
jokes.punchline: Lists mundane automated kiosks instead of impressive credits
jokes.explanation: Classic Troy McClure format applied to increasingly mundane 'credits.' Perfect character consistency with good specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as... the ATM at the Springfield Bank, the self-checkout machine at the Kwik-E-Mart, and the automated parking meter on Evergreen Terrace.

jokes.text: It says the ceiling was donated by the Velcro company.
jokes.setup: Museum visitors stuck to ceiling after Velcro interaction
jokes.punchline: Homer reads sign explaining the ceiling donation while stuck to it
jokes.explanation: Great visual setup and payoff. The corporate donation aspect adds layer to the physical comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This ceiling was donated by the Burlington Coat Factory.

jokes.text: And the floor was donated by American SuperBall.
jokes.setup: Following the Velcro ceiling gag
jokes.punchline: Explains bouncy chaos happening on SuperBall floor
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the corporate donation theme. Sets up bouncy floor visual comedy effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my God, what's happening?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't know, but it's awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The whole house is bouncing! We're all going to die!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts, gravity!

jokes.text: Ovulate, damn you. Ovulate! - You are out of sperm.
jokes.setup: Homer using reproductive education computer
jokes.punchline: Desperate command followed by clinical rejection
jokes.explanation: Perfect combination of Homer's frustration with clinical computer response. Edgy but character-appropriate humor.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, work with me here!

dialogue.speaker: Computer
dialogue.line: Does not compute.

jokes.text: Whoa! This whole planet is like one big skate park!
jokes.setup: Bart exploring space exhibit with low gravity simulation
jokes.punchline: Reduces cosmic wonder to skateboarding terms
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart perspective - seeing everything through his interests. Good character integration reducing space exploration to skate culture.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, zero gravity! This is so cool, man.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm like, totally catching air right now.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: This rules! It's like the ultimate kickflip!

jokes.text: I found a moon rock in my nose. Houston, we have a booger.
jokes.setup: Ralph exploring space exhibit
jokes.punchline: Transforms famous NASA quote into gross-out humor via booger discovery
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay combining famous NASA quote with Ralph's innocent grossness. Perfect character voice and excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Wow, look at all this booger.

jokes.text: Hey, loser, this planet's for space studs. Yeah, blast off. I know you. My daddy took your beer.
jokes.setup: Bullies picking on Ralph in space exhibit
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent reference to Chief Wiggum confiscating their alcohol
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ralph's innocent honesty about his father's police work disrupting the bullies' tough talk.

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: Hey, look at the retard playing with the rocket ship.

dialogue.speaker: Kearney
dialogue.line: Let's pants him!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: My dad's the Chief of Police. He'll arrest you for... for...

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: He confiscated all your beer.

jokes.text: You poor little boy! - Ah, no. He'll be fine. He's always getting himself stuffed into this or that.
jokes.setup: Marge shows concern for Ralph being bullied
jokes.punchline: Chief Wiggum casually dismisses his son's regular victimization
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Chief Wiggum's oblivious parenting. Casual tone about son's suffering is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Chief Wiggum, I'm worried about Ralph. The other children are always picking on him.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Marge, my boy builds character through a little rough and tumble. Why, I was bullied as a kid and look how I turned out!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: ...That's not really helping your case.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Besides, it keeps him humble. Aw, he's a tough little guy. Tougher than his old man, probably.

jokes.text: Yeah, he's just playing Wiggle Puppy. That's a dog he made up who flies by wagging his tail.
jokes.setup: Explaining Ralph's strange behavior
jokes.punchline: Detailed description of Ralph's bizarre imaginary friend
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Ralph's innocent imagination. 'Wiggle Puppy' is both absurd and endearingly childlike. Great specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, it all started when Ralph invented an imaginary friend named Bork.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Bork lives in my closet and he tells me to do bad things, but he's fun!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: That's not an imaginary friend, Ralph. That's just your brain talking to itself.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: My brain talks to me too! It tells me that I'm a bus driver and Principal Skinner is actually my wife.

jokes.text: Ah, yeah, the kid's incredible. I mean, the special schools are all over him.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests Ralph has a lot of imagination
jokes.punchline: Chief Wiggum misinterprets special education interest as achievement
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Chief Wiggum's obliviousness to his son's developmental needs. Dark irony delivered innocently.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Son, your mother says you've got a lot of imagination. That means you're gonna do real good in Special Ed.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Yay!

jokes.text: Sure, we'd all love some real friends, Marge... but what are the odds of that happening?
jokes.setup: Marge suggests Ralph needs real friends
jokes.punchline: Chief Wiggum's pessimistic assessment of friendship prospects
jokes.explanation: Beautifully melancholy line revealing Chief Wiggum's own loneliness while being darkly realistic about Ralph's social prospects.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Real friends? Have you seen the way other kids treat him? They call him names, they laugh at him, they exclude him from games. No, ma'am, Ralph doesn't have real friends, and he never will.

jokes.text: Your hair is tall and pretty.
jokes.setup: Ralph responding to Marge's kindness
jokes.punchline: Innocent misuse of 'tall' to describe Marge's distinctive hair
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph-ism - technically accurate but awkward phrasing. Perfect character voice and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: You're a tall lady!

jokes.text: Help! She's touching my special area!
jokes.setup: Marge gently touches Ralph's hair
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent misinterpretation creates uncomfortable implication
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe humor from Ralph's innocent misunderstanding of appropriate language. Creates awkwardness from pure intentions.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Your hair is so soft, Ralph.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Me fail English? That's unpossible.

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo! Beer, beer, beer! Bed, bed, bed!
jokes.setup: Chief Wiggum's suggestion about beer and bed
jokes.punchline: Ralph's enthusiastic chanting about both concepts equally
jokes.explanation: Ralph's innocent enthusiasm for concepts he doesn't understand. Good follow-up to previous dark parenting joke.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Yay! Beer and bed! Beer and bed! Beer and bed!

jokes.text: You would know. - Bart, you don't have to go anywhere today. I've got a surprise for you. - What? - I scheduled a playdate.
jokes.setup: Bart insults Ralph, Marge offers him a surprise
jokes.punchline: The 'surprise' is forced socialization with the kid he just insulted
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup where Marge's 'surprise' is actually Bart's punishment. Well-structured comedic timing.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, I have a surprise for you!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Ralph is coming over to play with you.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No way, Mom! I just told him his intelligence could charitably be described as 'room temperature.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, you're going to spend the afternoon together and you're going to be nice about it.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, that's not a surprise, that's a punishment.

jokes.text: A what? With who? - [Gasps] - Hi, Bart. We're gonna be friends.
jokes.setup: Bart's horror at the playdate concept
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent enthusiasm contrasts with Bart's dread
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast - Ralph's innocent joy versus Bart's social horror. Good cringe setup for episode conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A playdate? Ew!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Yay! We can play tag and eat cupcakes and play tag again!

jokes.text: I have a finger trap. Whoa. Get him off. Get him off! Fighting only makes it tighter.
jokes.setup: Ralph shows Bart a finger trap toy
jokes.punchline: Bart gets trapped and panics while Ralph calmly explains the principle
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy metaphor for Bart being trapped in the friendship. Ralph's calm wisdom contrasts with Bart's panic.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm learnding!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Ralph? A little help here?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: The more you struggle, the tighter it gets.

jokes.text: The social order of elementary school is densely layered. The coolest kids are at the top- - Oh, you mean the 'A' students?
jokes.setup: Bart explains school social hierarchy to Marge
jokes.punchline: Marge misinterprets coolness as academic achievement
jokes.explanation: Perfect generational divide humor. Bart's sophisticated social analysis versus Marge's innocent academic assumptions.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, you don't understand. Being cool isn't about grades. It's about not caring about grades.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh! So you're saying the coolest kids are the ones who get straight A's without even trying?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No, Mom. The coolest kids are the ones who get F's and don't care.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Then we need to get you more tutoring right away!

jokes.text: I can't hear you! [Humming]
jokes.setup: Bart tries to protest the playdate arrangement
jokes.punchline: Marge childishly avoids discussion by humming loudly
jokes.explanation: Role reversal humor where Marge acts more childishly than Bart. Simple but effective avoidance tactic comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, I don't want to go on a playdate with Ralph!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, you're going and that's final.

jokes.text: Your toys are fun to touch. Mine are all sticky.
jokes.setup: Ralph examining Bart's toys
jokes.punchline: Innocent revelation about poor toy hygiene
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph oversharing about gross domestic conditions. Innocent delivery makes it endearing rather than just disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Wow, this action figure hasn't been washed in weeks!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, he's been through a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm eating his dirt.

jokes.text: I dropped my 'Popsticle' in your toy chest.
jokes.setup: Ralph explains why his toys are sticky
jokes.punchline: Mispronunciation of 'Popsicle' while creating mess
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph speech pattern with the made-up 'Popsticle.' Shows his innocent destructiveness and linguistic creativity.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm helping!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: The Popsicles are all melty. I call them Popsickles!

jokes.text: Shh! I been here two hours, and Bart still hasn't finded me.
jokes.setup: Bart suggests hide-and-seek to get rid of Ralph
jokes.punchline: Ralph has been hiding for hours while Bart wasn't even looking
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph grammar ('finded') and innocent dedication to a game Bart abandoned. Shows both Ralph's commitment and Bart's neglect.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Ralph, wanna play hide-and-seek?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Okay!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Great, you go hide.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm hiding! I'm hiding!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. I'll look for you... later.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: *after hours of hiding* Ready or not, here I come! ...Bart? ...Bart? Why aren't you looking for me?

jokes.text: But people will see me paired up with a doofus. You have no idea what that's like.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests they go outside
jokes.punchline: Bart complains to Marge about being seen with a social outcast
jokes.explanation: Great irony as Bart complains to his own mother about social embarrassment. Self-awareness without actually understanding the irony.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why don't you go outside and play?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Outside? Are you kidding? Everyone will see me with a social outcast!

jokes.text: Slow down, Bart. My legs don't know how to be as long as yours.
jokes.setup: Ralph trying to keep up with Bart walking
jokes.punchline: Bizarre explanation for height difference using 'legs don't know'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Ralph-ism anthropomorphizing his legs as having their own knowledge. Perfect innocent absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: My legs don't know each other very well.

jokes.text: Videotaping this crime spree is the best idea we ever had!
jokes.setup: Bullies engaging in criminal behavior
jokes.punchline: Enthusiastically documenting evidence of their own crimes
jokes.explanation: Perfect criminal stupidity joke. The enthusiasm for self-incrimination shows classic teenage short-sightedness.

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: Hey, check it out! I'm filming us stealing this car for my TikTok!

dialogue.speaker: Dolph
dialogue.line: Dude, you're literally recording our faces committing grand theft auto.

dialogue.speaker: Kearney
dialogue.line: Yeah man, we're gonna go viral for sure—right before we go to jail.

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'll use a bunch of hashtags. #CriminalLife #SpringfieldBoys

jokes.text: Bushes are nice 'cause they don't have prickers. Unless they do. This one did. Ouch.
jokes.setup: Ralph and Bart hiding in bushes
jokes.punchline: Ralph's circular logic immediately disproven by experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph logic - makes a general statement, immediately contradicts it, then proves the contradiction. Great circular reasoning structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: If we stay very still, they won't see us.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: We're not in danger. We're in a bush.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Exactly. They can't see us.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm in danger.

jokes.text: That's where I saw the leprechaun. - Right. A leprechaun. - He told me to burn things.
jokes.setup: Ralph points out deep end of pool as forbidden area
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of hallucinatory arson instructions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from innocent leprechaun sighting to pyromaniac hallucinations. Perfect Ralph delivery makes it both disturbing and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: You can't go in the deep end. It's for big kids.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What's the big deal? I can swim.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: My mom says it's dangerous. She also says the voices tell me to set fires.

jokes.text: Did he say I'm not allowed in there? - Yes. - Well, I'm going in anyway.
jokes.setup: Ralph says his dad forbids him from the closet
jokes.punchline: Bart finds loophole in the literal wording
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's manipulative logic and rule-bending nature. Simple but effective setup for trouble.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: My dad says I can't go in the closet.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: He said you can't go in the closet, he didn't say you can't go in the hallway closet.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Bart, you're a genius!

jokes.text: Wow! Dad's been in jail six times! Aw, Mom's only been in twice.
jokes.setup: Ralph looking through police files
jokes.punchline: Casual family criminality statistics with disappointed tone about mom
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor with Ralph's innocent disappointment that mom is less accomplished at crime. Perfect economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Wow, look at all these criminal records. Dad's been arrested 52 times, Bart's wanted in three states, Lisa's got outstanding warrants... My mom's never even gotten a parking ticket.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm really disappointed in you, Mom.

jokes.text: A 341 in my own bedroom. All right, crookie. Let's dance.
jokes.setup: Chief Wiggum discovers intruders in his room
jokes.punchline: Uses police codes and tough talk before immediately getting hurt
jokes.explanation: Classic Chief Wiggum incompetence. Professional police language followed by immediate physical failure. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Alright, we got a 10-31 in progress. All units converge on my position. You're looking at 20 years to life, pal.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: I'm gonna take you down hard, you hear me? I've been on the force for...

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: AHHHHH!

jokes.text: What is your fascination with my forbidden closet of mystery?
jokes.setup: Chief Wiggum asking Ralph about the off-limits closet
jokes.punchline: Dramatically overblown description of ordinary police storage
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Chief Wiggum's tendency toward dramatic language for mundane things. 'Forbidden closet of mystery' is perfectly overwrought.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Ralph, you know you're not supposed to go in that closet.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: But Chief, what's in there?

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Son, that closet contains the most dangerous, deadly, and classified materials known to law enforcement. Weapons of mass destruction. Classified government documents. And... my collection of old donuts.

jokes.text: It's on the house.
jokes.setup: Chief Wiggum gives kids riot gear
jokes.punchline: Treats police equipment like restaurant hospitality
jokes.explanation: Perfect casual misuse of service industry language for dangerous police equipment. Shows Chief Wiggum's misplaced priorities.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: All right, kids, put these on. They're riot gear.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Wow, Dad! This is cool!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now, who wants some complimentary riot helmets?

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Me!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Can I get you gentlemen anything else? More pepper spray? A breadstick?

jokes.text: That's Daddy's magic key. It opens every door in town.
jokes.setup: Bart asks about strange key
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent description of police master key as 'magic'
jokes.explanation: Good setup for plot development. Ralph's childlike interpretation of official police equipment as 'magic' is perfectly innocent.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Ralph, what's that key?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: It's a magic key! My dad says it opens any door. He uses it all the time at work.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Your dad's a cop, right?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Uh-huh. He says it's magic because it makes people real happy... right before they go to jail.

jokes.text: This is Jerry Maguire. Show me the message. Show me the message!
jokes.setup: Lisa recording answering machine message
jokes.punchline: Mangled movie quote adapted for voicemail
jokes.explanation: Dated pop culture reference without much twist. Shows Lisa's attempt at being current but lacks comedic sophistication.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You've reached the Simpson residence. I can't come to the phone right now, I'm busy living my life. Leave a message after the beep.

jokes.text: This is Geraldine, and the devil made me miss your call... so here come the beep. Here come the beep!
jokes.setup: Lisa trying another answering machine message
jokes.punchline: Blames Satan for missing calls, announces beep dramatically
jokes.explanation: Better than first attempt - more absurd and creative. The devil excuse and beep announcement show Lisa's creative side.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Hi, you've reached the Simpson residence. I'm sorry I can't take your call right now. Satan is making me miss all my messages. Please leave your name and number after the beep.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: *BEEP*

jokes.text: The world is our toy store. - Toy store, toy store, toy store! Whee!
jokes.setup: Bart explains their new freedom with the master key
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent enthusiasm and repetition
jokes.explanation: Ralph's childlike excitement and repetitive speech pattern. Shows his inability to grasp the magnitude of their situation.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: With this master key, we can go anywhere in the school. We're free, man!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Everywhere?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Everywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Even the girls' bathroom?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, sure, why not.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Oh boy! Oh boy! Oh boy!

jokes.text: Check it out, guys. It's dork and dorker.
jokes.setup: Bullies spot Bart and Ralph together
jokes.punchline: Play on 'Dumb and Dumber' with social hierarchy insults
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay on movie title adapted to school social dynamics. Effective bully language but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: Hey, look who it is — Dumb and Dumber!

dialogue.speaker: Kearney
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you're both of 'em!

dialogue.speaker: Dolph
dialogue.line: Nah man, Ralph's dumber. Bart just hangs out with him.

jokes.text: We're on a playdate. - [Laughing] - Isn't that adorable? Ah, the girls are on a playdate!
jokes.setup: Bart tries to explain his situation to bullies
jokes.punchline: The term 'playdate' makes him sound more childish and feminine
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to earlier setup about the embarrassing terminology. Shows how adult language doesn't translate to kid social dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Look, guys, I'm not trying to avoid you. I just have a playdate scheduled.

dialogue.speaker: Bullies
dialogue.line: A playdate? What are you, five?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's not like that. My mom set it up with—

dialogue.speaker: Bullies
dialogue.line: Did you just say your mommy set it up? And you're going on a playdate? That's the most pathetic thing we've ever heard.

jokes.text: Um, we're just hanging, chilling, little bit of illing.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to sound cool
jokes.punchline: Makes up 'illing' to complete the rhyme scheme
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's desperation to sound cool leading to nonsense words

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yo, check it out, man. I'm chillin', I'm illin', I'm straight up killin' it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's 'illin''?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh... it's cool, Dad. Like chillin' and illin', you know?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not a word, boy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Is too! It means... uh... to be really... illing.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? Would a lame wad have the police master key?
jokes.setup: Bullies call Bart a 'lame wad'
jokes.punchline: Bart reveals powerful contraband to defend his reputation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's desperation for social acceptance leading him to reveal criminal capability.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lame wad? That's the best you got? Check this out.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I got a Radioactive Man comic book, first edition, still in the plastic.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Whoa!

dialogue.speaker: Dolph
dialogue.line: That's so cool!

dialogue.speaker: Kearney
dialogue.line: You're not a lame wad anymore, man.

jokes.text: Here she is- the big house. The stony lonesome. The thug jug. The mobster trap. - Penn State. The old crook-
jokes.setup: Bart dramatically describes the prison with classic nicknames
jokes.punchline: Nelson adds 'Penn State' as if it's another prison nickname
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of prison nicknames with Nelson's unexpected addition of an actual college. Shows kids' limited knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The big house, the slammer, the joint, the clink, the pen...

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Penn State.

jokes.text: I thought we were friends. - [Laughing] Wait. I missed that. Get him to say it again.
jokes.setup: Ralph expresses hurt at Bart's betrayal
jokes.punchline: Bullies find his emotional honesty so funny they want to hear it repeated
jokes.explanation: Dark moment showing bullies' cruelty in finding innocent emotional expression entertaining. Ralph's sincerity makes it more painful.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: You were my only friend, Bart. How could you betray me like this?

dialogue.speaker: Kearney
dialogue.line: Dude, that was so funny! Do it again!

dialogue.speaker: Dolph
dialogue.line: Yeah, say the part about being betrayed again!

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: We're totally gonna make him say sad stuff all the time now.

jokes.text: I can't bail on a friend, even if it is Ralph.
jokes.setup: Bart realizes he needs to help Ralph
jokes.punchline: Shows moral growth while still insulting Ralph
jokes.explanation: Good character development moment. Shows Bart's moral center emerging despite his continued social prejudices about Ralph.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Ralph, I know we haven't always gotten along, but I'm gonna help you out.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Yay! Thanks, Bart!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Don't get excited. I'm only doing this because my conscience won't shut up. And between you and me, you're still a complete idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm helping!

jokes.text: Yeah, well, I hope the irony's not lost on you, Simpson.
jokes.setup: Bart uses Ralph's friendship plea against the bullies
jokes.punchline: Bully demonstrates unexpected vocabulary about dramatic irony
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion where the bully shows literary sophistication. The formal language contrasts perfectly with his thuggish behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: Wait, that's ironic.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, Jimbo, that's what irony is.

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: No, no, I know what irony is. It's like rain on your wedding day.

jokes.text: I know. Let's go pick some huckleberries! - Yeah! - Huckleberries! All right!
jokes.setup: Bullies need new activity after prison break-in fails
jokes.punchline: Tough delinquents enthusiastically embrace wholesome berry-picking
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contradiction - tough bullies getting excited about innocent rural activity. Great subversion of expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha! We need a new criminal enterprise after that prison break-in fiasco.

dialogue.speaker: Bullies
dialogue.line: Yeah, what should we do?

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: I got it! Let's go berry-picking!

dialogue.speaker: Bullies
dialogue.line: Yeah! Berry-picking rules!

jokes.text: The pointy kitty took it!
jokes.setup: Key gets taken by rat in prison
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent misidentification of rat as 'pointy kitty'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph-ism describing a rat as 'pointy kitty.' Shows his innocent way of processing scary or unpleasant things.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Look, a pointy kitty!

jokes.text: Smells like hot dogs.
jokes.setup: Bart says electric chair smells like justice
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent food association with death machine
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Ralph's innocent association. The contrast between death and food makes it both disturbing and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The electric chair smells like justice.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Mmm, justice.

jokes.text: Till death do you part.
jokes.setup: Bart puts wedding cake couple in electric chair
jokes.punchline: Sinister interpretation of marriage vows through electrocution
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor connecting wedding vows to execution. Perfect structure and economy with genuinely clever wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Till death do us part!

dialogue.speaker: Electric Chair
dialogue.line: *BZZZZZZT*

jokes.text: Why do I always shout first? Just gives them a chance to run away. Well, I'm an idiot.
jokes.setup: Guard shouts warning before investigating
jokes.punchline: Self-aware analysis of his tactical incompetence
jokes.explanation: Great self-awareness humor. Character recognizes his own professional incompetence in matter-of-fact way.

dialogue.speaker: Guard
dialogue.line: Halt! Who goes there?

dialogue.speaker: Guard
dialogue.line: I should probably investigate this alone, without backup or a radio call. What could go wrong? I'll just walk slowly toward the dark area where the suspicious noise came from. This is definitely the smart tactical approach.

dialogue.speaker: Guard
dialogue.line: You know, in retrospect, I realize I'm a security guard with zero training who makes terrible decisions that always end badly. But I keep doing the exact same thing every single time.

jokes.text: Got any threes? - Go fish. - Oh! See, here's the problem, Ralph. You have several threes. - Go fish!
jokes.setup: Playing Go Fish card game
jokes.punchline: Ralph doesn't understand rules despite having the requested cards
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph character moment showing his inability to grasp simple game rules even when explained.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Got any twos?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Go fish.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ralph, you're holding three twos.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you said 'got any shoes.' I don't got any shoes.

jokes.text: Hey, that's our play chair!
jokes.setup: Seeing electric chair on TV
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent identification of death device
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - Ralph's innocent claim of ownership over execution device

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: That's where they make the electric chair look so cool!

jokes.text: He's gonna smell like hot dogs.
jokes.setup: Mayor about to be electrocuted in chair
jokes.punchline: Ralph recalls his earlier food association with electrocution
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier joke. Ralph's consistent innocent association between death and food maintains character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I bent my wookie.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Wait, I think it's supposed to be 'I bent my Wookiee.' Oh, that still doesn't work.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go find a cash machine.

jokes.text: Lisa's a good thinker.
jokes.setup: Bart struggling to think of solution to save mayor
jokes.punchline: Ralph's simple, accurate assessment of Lisa's intelligence
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character moment. Ralph occasionally shows surprising wisdom in his innocent observations.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Lisa's a genius!

jokes.text: Lisa's a good thinker. That's it. Ralph, you're a genius!
jokes.setup: Bart needing to think of solution
jokes.punchline: Calling Ralph a genius for suggesting Lisa
jokes.explanation: Decent ironic praise - calling Ralph genius for obvious suggestion

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, that's it! Ralph, you're a genius!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Me? A genius? Aw, thanks, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa! That's the answer!

jokes.text: Smithers, there's a rocket in my pocket. - You don't have to tell me, sir.
jokes.setup: Lisa's warning rocket lands in Burns' pocket
jokes.punchline: Smithers' weary acknowledgment of Burns' suggestive phrasing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns-Smithers dynamic with sexual innuendo. Smithers' tired response suggests this happens regularly.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, a rocket has landed in my pocket.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. Will you be needing anything?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Ehhh, why don't you reach in and get it out for me?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Of course, sir.

jokes.text: Then the old prison has been getting free electricity for 30 years. I'll put a stop to that.
jokes.setup: Discovering electric chair is still powered
jokes.punchline: Burns prioritizes stopping minor electrical theft over human safety
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns character moment showing his warped priorities. Focuses on minor cost-saving while mayor is in danger.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent! The chair is still operational. Now we can finally execute the condemned prisoner.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, the electric chair hasn't been used in decades. Perhaps we should focus on more pressing matters?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense, Smithers! Do you know how much electricity that chair wastes when it's on standby? We're hemorrhaging money! That's my biggest concern here.

jokes.text: But the rocket was my idea. - But asking you was Ralph's idea. - You're the man, Ralph!
jokes.setup: Bart wants credit for the rescue plan
jokes.punchline: Lisa gives Ralph credit for the inspiration, crowd agrees
jokes.explanation: Good character resolution showing Ralph finally getting recognition while Bart learns about sharing credit.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, what about me? I came up with the whole plan!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, Ralph's innocent optimism was the real inspiration. We couldn't have done it without his faith in us.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Yay!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Hip hip hooray for Ralph!

jokes.text: Ah, you've done grand, laddie. Now you know what you have to do- burn the house down! Burn 'em all!
jokes.setup: Ralph being celebrated as hero
jokes.punchline: Hallucinatory leprechaun returns with arson instructions
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to earlier disturbing joke. The timing after Ralph's moment of glory makes it perfectly subversive.

dialogue.speaker: Leprechaun
dialogue.line: Follow the magic!

dialogue.speaker: Leprechaun
dialogue.line: Now to be settin' fires, ye'll be needin' some gasoline and a book of matches.

tmdb_overview: At Marge’s insistence, Bart has a “play date” with Ralph. Bart suddenly sees the upside of this friendship when he discovers Chief Wiggum’s “master key” and goes to explore Springfield’s abandoned prison.
insight: Ralph's incompetence drives 19 jokes—lowest-density episode masks character-focused humor depth.
